title: Iran FM Araghchi warns Bushehr nuclear plant strikes would 'end life in GCC capitals, not Tehran'
content:
Russia — which has been involved in constructing and operating the facility — announced it was evacuating 198 personnel from the site.

Bushehr nuclear power plant (AP file photo)

US and Israeli strikes hit near Iran's Bushehr nuclear facility

Iran warns radioactive fallout could threaten Gulf capitals

Russia evacuates staff; IAEA urges nuclear sites not be attacked Did our AI summary help?

Strikes by the United States and Israel targeted an area near Iran’s Bushehr nuclear power facility on Saturday, drawing sharp warnings from Iranian officials about potential regional consequences. Iran foreign minister Abbas Araghchi cautioned that continued attacks could trigger radioactive fallout that would disproportionately affect neighbouring Gulf states rather than Iran itself, reported AFP.

He warned that such a scenario could "end life in GCC (Gulf Cooperation Council) capitals, not Tehran", underlining the plant’s proximity to countries such as Kuwait, Bahrain and Qatar, which lie significantly closer to Bushehr than the Iranian capital.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The strike reportedly resulted in the death of a security guard. In response, Russia — which has been involved in constructing and operating the facility — announced it was evacuating 198 personnel from the site.

The International Atomic Energy Agency also reacted with concern. Its chief, Rafael Grossi, said on X that while no spike in radiation had been detected, the situation remained alarming. He described the incident as the fourth of its kind in recent weeks and stressed that "NPP (nuclear power plant) sites or nearby areas must never be attacked."

Elsewhere, fresh strikes were reported in Tehran, where an AFP journalist observed dense grey smoke blanketing parts of the skyline. Residents described a worsening humanitarian and economic strain. "Things are really hard right now. You can't even plan six hours ahead," a 40-year-old photographer told AFP, adding, "There's really nothing we can do. We don't have the power to change the situation," as he sold personal belongings online to cope.

The escalation comes as search operations continue for a missing American airman after a jet crash over Iran. Meanwhile, Tehran announced new offensives, with the Revolutionary Guards claiming responsibility for a drone attack on a commercial vessel in Bahrain allegedly linked to Israel.

The broader conflict began over a month ago following U.S.-Israeli strikes that killed Iran’s supreme leader, Ali Khamenei, setting off retaliatory actions that have since expanded across West Asia and disrupted the global economy.

title: 'Another scandal for Americans': Iran alleges US 'plotting' to kill missing pilot
content:
US fighter jets reportedly carried out strikes in parts of Kohgiluyeh city in Kohgiluyeh and Boyer-Ahmad Province, believing the missing crew member was in the area.

The source cited in the report further claimed that Washington was “not telling the whole truth” about the incident, including its assertion that one crew member had already been rescued. (Representative image)

Iran claims US is trying to kill, not rescue, missing pilot

US helicopters fired on by tribesmen during search operations

Iran offers cash rewards for info on missing American crew member Did our AI summary help?

The U.S. is attempting to kill, rather than rescue, a pilot from a fighter jet shot down over Iran two days ago, according to Iran’s semi-official Tasnim News Agency, which cited a military source.

“After their disappointment to rescue the pilot… the Americans are seeking to bomb and kill [him] in locations where he is likely to be present,” the source has been cited by Tasnim.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The report, released on Sunday, alleges that US fighter jets carried out strikes in parts of Kohgiluyeh city in Kohgiluyeh and Boyer-Ahmad Province, believing the missing crew member was in the area.

The source cited in the report further claimed that Washington was “not telling the whole truth” about the incident, including its assertion that one crew member had already been rescued.

“The story of the pilots… will become another scandal for the Americans,” the source said, without providing evidence. It remained unclear whether the missing individual was in Iranian custody.

Meanwhile, according to Iranian state-linked media, groups of local tribesmen fired at US helicopters involved in search operations for the missing crew member.

According to Iran’s semi-official Fars News Agency, two Black Hawk helicopters came under fire in remote mountainous areas of Kohgiluyeh and Boyer-Ahmad province and the Bakhtiari region.

The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) praised the tribesmen as “courageous, valiant and victorious guardians of the borders.” Footage released by Fars purportedly showed armed Bakhtiari tribesmen searching mountainous terrain for the missing American.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

“God willing, he will be found,” one of the men said in the video.

Iranian authorities have also announced cash rewards for information leading to the capture of the missing crew member, intensifying efforts on the ground.

At the same time, the IRGC highlighted what it described as a newly deployed air defence system, claiming it was used to shoot down the fighter jet and target US helicopters involved in rescue operations.

“We will certainly achieve full control over the skies of our country and will prove the enemy’s humiliation to the world more than ever before,” a spokesperson for the Central Khatam al-Anbiya Headquarters said in a video released by Tasnim.

US Central Command has not publicly responded to the late

title: ‘He knows he could be next’: Trump firings rattle Pentagon as Pete Hegseth faces ouster fears, Signalgate fallout
content:
US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth has ordered a sweeping leadership shake-up amid the ongoing Iran conflict, including the resignation of Army Chief of Staff Randy George and the removal of other senior officials. The Pentagon has offered little explanation beyond saying it was “time for a leadership change.”

Pete Hegseth

US Defence Secretary Hegseth orders Pentagon leadership shake-up

Tensions rise between Hegseth and Army Secretary Driscoll

Shake-up linked to fallout from 2025 'Signalgate' controversy Did our AI summary help?

Recent high-profile firings in US President Donald Trump’s administration, including that of former Attorney General Pam Bondi, have triggered fresh uncertainty within the government, with tensions now spilling into the Pentagon, according to a report by The New York Post.

US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth has ordered a sweeping leadership shake-up amid the ongoing Iran conflict, including the resignation of Army Chief of Staff Randy George and the removal of other senior officials. The Pentagon has offered little explanation beyond saying it was “time for a leadership change.”

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Rift at the top

At the centre of the turmoil is Hegseth’s strained relationship with Army Secretary Dan Driscoll. Sources told the publication that tensions between the two have been building for months, with Driscoll seen as a potential successor.

One source claimed the White House has warned Hegseth against removing Driscoll for now, while another said the defence secretary is increasingly concerned about being replaced.

“[Hegseth] is very concerned about being fired… and he knows that Driscoll is one of the top contenders,” a source said, adding that recent actions appear aimed at officials perceived to be aligned with Driscoll.

‘Paranoia’ after Signalgate

Officials also linked the latest shake-up to fallout from the 2025 ‘Signalgate’ controversy, in which a journalist was mistakenly added to a sensitive national security group chat.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

“This is all driven by the insecurity and paranoia that Pete has developed since Signalgate,” one official told the New York Post.

Tensions reportedly escalated further after US Vice President JD Vance tasked Driscoll with a role in Ukraine negotiations, a move that sources said “got under Hegseth’s skin.”

Optics and internal fallout

The timing of the shake-up has raised concerns within the administration, particularly as Trump has publicly maintained that the Iran conflict is progressing well.

“This undermines completely the tone and the message that the president wants to send,” one source said.

Despite the unrest, there is no immediate indication that Trump plans to remove either Hegseth or Driscoll.

Succession chatter

Speculation over potential replacements has already begun. Hegseth’s spokesperson Sean Parnell has emerged as a possible contender for Army secretary, though officials say he remains foc

title: First Iraqi tanker passes through Hormuz under Tehran’s exemptions
content:
An Iraqi oil tanker carrying crude passed through the Strait of Hormuz after Iran granted Baghdad a special shipping exemption.

Iraqi oil tanker transits Strait of Hormuz after Iran exemption

An oil tanker carrying crude from Iraq appeared to be transiting the Strait of Hormuz chokepoint, a day after Iran said its neighbor had special exemption to use the waterway.

The Suezmax Ocean Thunder picked up its cargo from Iraq’s Basrah terminal in early March and is now bound for Malaysia, according to tanker-tracking data compiled by Bloomberg. Such vessels can carry about 1 million barrels of crude. The Asian nation has also received dispensation to use the strait, which is blocked for most other countries.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The Iranian military said Saturday it had granted an exemption on shipping restrictions to “brotherly Iraq,” though it remained unclear how this would be implemented in practice. At the end of March, Malaysia said its tankers had been given permission to cross the vital sea corridor.

Tracking data show the Ocean Thunder traversing the strait via a narrow northerly route that takes ships between the Iranian islands of Larak and Qeshm. Recent recorded transits have taken that passage apparently approved by Tehran.

Tracking vessel movements can be an inexact science because of the potential for electronic interference with ships’ signals and the intentional disablement of transponders by pilots sailing through risky zones.

Oil traders have been closely watching the strait since it was effectively shuttered shortly after the US and Israel launched attacks on the Islamic Republic on Feb. 28. The closure has bolstered prices of crude oil and products such as jet fuel and diesel, threatening financial pain for consumers everywhere.

title: Trump says airman ‘seriously wounded’ as Iran was ‘getting close’ before rescue
content:
Trump says a seriously wounded US airman was rescued from Iran in a rare high-risk operation as Iranian forces were closing in.

Trump says airman was ‘seriously wounded,’ was rescued as Iranian military closed in

US President Donald Trump said a US airman rescued from inside Iran after his warplane was downed was “seriously wounded,” and announced he would brief the media on the operation.

In a post on Truth Social, Trump said, “We have rescued the seriously wounded, and really brave, F-15 Crew Member/Officer, from deep inside the mountains of Iran.” He added that Iranian forces had been searching in large numbers and “getting close” before US troops carried out the extraction.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Trump had earlier described the airman as “injured” and “safe and sound,” but later clarified the seriousness of his condition while praising the mission as highly complex. “This type of raid is seldom attempted because of the danger to 'man and equipment.'

It just doesn’t happen!” he said. He also noted that the second rescue operation followed an earlier mission in which the pilot was recovered in broad daylight, calling both efforts unusual and high risk.

The rescue was conducted by US Special Operations forces during a nighttime mission in mountainous terrain after an extended search behind enemy lines. The first crew member had been recovered earlier, while the second remained stranded for hours before being located and extracted.

Describing the mission as a major success, Trump said, “An AMAZING show of bravery and talent by all!” and confirmed he would hold a news conference with military officials at the Oval Office to provide further details.

title: ‘Open the F***in Strait, you crazy ba*ds’: Trump warns Iran of strikes on power plants, bridges
content:
Trump warns Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz or face strikes, as he confirms a “seriously wounded” US airman was rescued from deep inside Iran in a high-risk mission.

Trump warns Iran of strikes on power plants, bridges in escalating threat

US President Donald Trump has issued a fresh warning to Iran, urging it to reopen the Strait of Hormuz or face severe consequences, as tensions in the region continue to escalate.

In a post on Truth Social, Trump said, “Tuesday will be Power Plant Day, and Bridge Day, all wrapped up in one, in Iran. There will be nothing like it!!! Open the Fuckin’ Strait, you crazy bastards, or you’ll be living in Hell - JUST WATCH! Praise be to Allah. President DONALD J. TRUMP.”

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The remarks come as Trump has repeatedly pressed Tehran to allow free passage through the crucial maritime route, signalling potential strikes on key infrastructure such as power plants and bridges if Iran does not comply.

Earlier, Trump had issued a 48-hour ultimatum to Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. In a social media post on March 26, he reminded Tehran that it had 10 days to either “make a deal” or “open up the Hormuz Strait,” warning that the deadline was nearing.

Trump had first threatened on March 21 to “obliterate" Iran’s power plants, starting with the largest, if the country did not “FULLY OPEN, WITHOUT THREAT, the Strait of Hormuz, within 48 HOURS."

Two days later, he said “very good and productive conversations" were underway with Iranian authorities and postponed any strikes on power plants for five days. The deadline was later extended to 8:00 pm Monday (0000 GMT Tuesday).

The warning comes as Trump also confirmed that US special forces rescued a service member who had been missing behind enemy lines after Iran downed an F-15E fighter jet. The airman was recovered following a search-and-rescue operation and is reported to be injured but in stable condition.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Trump also said a seriously wounded US airman has been rescued from deep inside Iran, describing the operation as a rare and high-risk mission carried out as Iranian forces were closing in.

title: How Israel’s intelligence helped US rescue airman in Iran?
content:
Israel provided intelligence support to help the US locate and rescue a stranded airman in Iran during a complex multi-day operation, officials said.

Israel aided US airman rescue in Iran with intelligence support, official says

Israel provided intelligence support to the United States during a high-risk operation to rescue a stranded US airman in Iran and paused its own strikes in the area to facilitate the mission, an Israeli security official told Reuters. The development comes after the US confirmed the rescue of both crew members of an F-15E fighter jet that had been shot down by Iranian forces.

US President Donald Trump announced the recovery, calling it “one of the most daring Search and Rescue Operations in U.S. History.” “WE GOT HIM!” Trump wrote on Truth Social, adding the officer is now “SAFE and SOUND” after being stranded “behind enemy lines in the treacherous mountains of Iran.”

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

He said the pilot was injured but “will be just fine,” and had taken refuge in mountainous terrain while US forces monitored his position “24 hours a day, and diligently planning for his rescue.”

US officials said the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) played a key role through a deception campaign, spreading misinformation inside Iran that the airman had already been moved overland.

The effort helped mislead Iranian forces and aided US teams in locating the airman. A US official described the situation as a “needle in a haystack” scenario, saying, “A courageous American hidden within a mountain crevice, undetectable by conventional means but revealed through CIA intelligence.”

The multi-day mission involved special forces on the ground, supported by dozens of aircraft and helicopters. Reports said US forces engaged Iranian units during the final extraction phase. To support the operation, US troops also established a temporary airstrip, where some aircraft became immobilised and were later destroyed before evacuation.

Israeli support reportedly included intelligence sharing and possible strikes, though officials denied claims that Israeli commandos were directly involved. Iranian state media later reported finding wreckage of US aircraft and claimed they had been shot down, a claim disputed by US officials.

title: Oman, Iran hold Hormuz talks as Trump’s 48-hour deadline looms
content:
Oman and Iran discuss ensuring safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz as US President Donald Trump’s 48-hour ultimatum to reopen the route nears.

Oman-Iran talks on Hormuz come as Trump’s ultimatum clock ticks

Oman and Iran have held high-level talks to explore options for maintaining the flow of maritime traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, as the key global shipping route remains effectively restricted due to the ongoing conflict in the region.

According to Omani state media, deputy foreign ministers from both countries met along with technical experts to discuss ways to ensure safe navigation through the strategic waterway.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

“Oman and the Islamic Republic of Iran held a meeting at the deputy ministers level in the foreign ministries of the two countries, with the attendance of specialists from both sides, during which the possible options were discussed regarding ensuring the smooth passage through the Strait of Hormuz,” the news agency posted on X.

“The experts from both sides put forward a number of visions and proposals regarding it,” it added.

Oman’s foreign ministry also confirmed the meeting, stating, “A meeting was held between the Sultanate of Oman and the Islamic Republic of Iran on Saturday, April 4, 2026, at the level of the undersecretaries of the foreign ministries of the two countries, attended by specialists from both sides.”

“The meeting discussed possible options for ensuring the smooth flow of transit through the Strait of Hormuz amid the conditions currently prevailing in the region. During the meeting, experts from both sides presented a number of visions and proposals that will be studied.”

The Strait of Hormuz, a vital route for global oil and liquefied natural gas shipments, has been largely restricted since the conflict escalated on February 28, with only limited commercial traffic allowed through Iranian-controlled channels.

The development comes as US President Donald Trump warned Tehran to reopen the waterway within 48 hours or face consequences. “Remember when I gave Iran ten days to MAKE A DEAL or OPEN UP THE HORMUZ STRAIT,” Trump wrote on Truth Social.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Iran has since allowed the passage of vessels carrying essential goods, along with some foreign-flagged ships, even as broader access to the strategic corridor remains constrained.

title: Iran says new air defence system downed US F-15E: What do we know?
content:
Iran claims its air defence downed US aircraft during rescue operations, marking first confirmed US losses inside Iran, as Washington says strikes have severely degraded Tehran’s missile capabilities.

Iran downs US jets amid escalating conflict

Iran on Saturday claimed that a newly deployed air defence system had successfully brought down US aircraft, as hostilities with American and Israeli forces continue to intensify.

The assertion came shortly after the United States confirmed that the second crew member of a downed F-15E fighter jet had been rescued. Tehran, however, said it had struck another US aircraft during the rescue operation. According to Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), the aircraft was targeted while searching for the missing officer in the southern region of Isfahan, as reported by Fars news agency.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The claim was echoed by Iran’s Khatam al-Anbiya joint military command on state media, Reuters reported. Officials said the system had been responsible for shooting down two US planes and would allow Iran to “definitely achieve full control” over its airspace.

Was the Third Khordad system used?

Reports suggest Iran may have deployed the Third Khordad missile system—a mobile, medium-range surface-to-air defence platform—to hit the F-15E Strike Eagle. According to The New York Times, the system could have been involved in the strike, while experts cited by ABC News pointed to the possible use of passive infrared detection systems. Such systems track aircraft without emitting radar signals, making them far more difficult for US jets to detect and evade.

F-15E crash

The F-15E, estimated to be worth around $31 million, is believed to have come down in Kohgiluyeh and Boyer-Ahmad province in southwestern Iran, close to the Iraqi border.

Both crew members managed to eject. One was rescued shortly after the incident on Friday, while the second was recovered on Sunday.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Rescue mission comes under fire

During a follow-up mission to locate the missing crew member, an A-10 Thunderbolt II—better known as the “Warthog”—was hit. The aircraft later crashed after entering Kuwaiti airspace, though the pilot ejected safely.

The incidents mark the first confirmed losses of US aircraft inside Iran since the conflict began on February 28.

So far, the US military says it has struck more than 12,300 targets during the war, claiming the campaign has degraded Iran’s missile and drone capabilities by over 90 per cent.

title: Iranian media claims 'several US aircraft destroyed' in Isfahan, denies success of pilot rescue operation
content:
US rescues both crew members of a downed F-15 in Iran, while Iranian media claim the rescue mission failed and show wreckage of a US aircraft.

Iran claims “several US aircraft destroyed” in Isfahan, denies success of pilot rescue operation

Iranian state media has shown wreckage of a US C-130 aircraft that reportedly took part in the rescue of a downed fighter pilot in southern Iran. The US said the plane became stuck and had to be destroyed, while Iranian outlets claimed it was hit and destroyed by Iranian forces.

The reports appear to counter the US success in recovering the missing crew member, a mission President Donald Trump described as involving “dozens of aircraft, armed with the most lethal weapons.” Trump said the airman “sustained injuries, but he will be just fine.” Analysts noted the rescue prevented the airman from becoming a “bargaining chip” for Tehran.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Iran’s Tasnim News Agency cited the military headquarters, claiming “several enemy American aircraft in the southern Isfahan region were destroyed by the warriors of Islam, and the pilot rescue operation failed.”

The report mentioned that two Black Hawk helicopters and a C-130 transport aircraft were hit and burning. Several Iranian outlets published videos showing smoking wreckage, though CNN could not verify their authenticity.

Tasnim also accused Trump of trying to “cover up his heavy defeat” by tweeting about the special operation. Iran’s Parliament Speaker, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, shared one of the images, saying “if the United States gets three more victories like this, it will be utterly ruined.”

The US confirmed that both crew members of the F-15E Strike Eagle were rescued after a multi-day operation. Trump called it “one of the most daring search and rescue operations in US history,” adding the second airman was found behind enemy lines with support from dozens of aircraft and special forces.

If the United States gets three more victories like this, it will be utterly ruined. 👏 pic.twitter.com/jutDghVrjz — محمدباقر قالیباف | MB Ghalibaf (@mb_ghalibaf) April 5, 2026

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

“This brave Warrior was behind enemy lines in the treacherous mountains of Iran, being hunted down by our enemies, who were getting closer and closer by the hour,” he said.

title: China questions US avoidance of word ‘war’ as missile strikes continue in Iran: How US legally declares war
content:
China says US missile strikes on Iran avoid the word “war,” raising legal questions, while US President Trump confirms missing pilot rescued “SAFE and SOUND” after evading Iranian forces.

Xinhua says US missile strikes on Iran raise legal questions

Xinhua, China’s official news agency, said the United States’ recent missile attacks on Iran “carefully avoid using the word ‘war,’” suggesting Washington is trying to bypass congressional oversight.

The agency added that under international law, such large-scale cross-border strikes clearly constitute an international armed conflict, raising serious questions about legality and accountability.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

How US declares war

According to the US Constitution (Article I, Section 8), only Congress has the power to formally declare war, requiring a majority vote in both the House and Senate, followed by the President's signature. However, the U.S. has not formally declared war since 1942.

A formal declaration involves a joint resolution passed by both chambers of Congress and signed by the President. The last formal declaration of war was against Japan in 1941, followed by declarations against Germany and Italy in 1942.

Missing pilot rescued

This comes as US President Donald Trump said that the missing service member whose F-15 jet was shot down over Iran is now “SAFE and SOUND” after a US forces rescue operation involving “dozens of aircraft,” President Trump said.

According to US officials cited by The New York Times, the airman survived for nearly two days in hostile territory, evading Iranian forces that were actively searching for him.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

“This brave Warrior was behind enemy lines in the treacherous mountains of Iran, being hunted down by our enemies, who were getting closer and closer by the hour,” Trump said.

After ejecting, the airman moved to higher ground and remained hidden, armed with little more than a pistol. He used a distress beacon, GPS tracker and secure communication equipment to stay in touch with US forces and help guide the rescue.

title: Why Pope Leo is pushing back against using religion to justify war
content:
As tensions rise in the Middle East, the new pope is emerging as a quiet but firm voice against turning faith into a reason for conflict.

Why Pope Leo is pushing back against using religion to justify war

Earlier this week, Pope Leo XIV did something popes don’t usually do. He referred directly to Donald Trump while speaking about the ongoing war with Iran.

When asked what he would say to leaders in Washington and Israel, his response was simple but pointed. He said he hoped Trump would find an “off-ramp” to end the conflict, stressing the need for negotiations over escalation.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

That alone was significant. Popes tend to speak in broader moral terms rather than addressing specific leaders. But this time, Leo seemed to be deliberately choosing language that would land directly in the political conversation, CNN reported.

It also highlighted a contrast that’s becoming harder to ignore. On one side is a pope who speaks cautiously but consistently about peace. On the other is a political leadership that is far more visible, assertive and willing to frame the conflict in strategic or even ideological terms.

Drawing a line between faith and war

What really sets Leo apart right now is where he’s choosing to push back. As the war narrative has evolved, some voices within the US establishment, including US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth, have framed the conflict in religious terms, suggesting it carries a kind of divine backing.

Leo has rejected that idea quite clearly.

In his Holy Week remarks, he emphasised that religion cannot be used to justify violence, pointing to the core Christian idea of peace. His message was direct without being confrontational: faith, in his view, cannot be invoked to legitimise war.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

That puts him on a different track from those trying to blend political strategy with religious language. And while he hasn’t escalated his tone, the consistency of his message is starting to stand out.

A more active role than expected

Leo isn’t known for being outspoken in a dramatic way. His style is measured, almost understated. But behind that, there’s a growing sense that he’s willing to step into global debates when he feels it’s necessary.

Over the past week, he has called for an “Easter truce,” spoken repeatedly about ending the conflict and even held conversations with world leaders, including those directly involved in ongoing wars.

What’s emerging is not a confrontational figure, but someone who is steadily positioning himself as a moral counterweight to escalating rhetoric.

The debate over what counts as a “just war”

His stance is also feeding into a deeper debate within the Catholic world.

The idea of a “just war” has long been part of Church teaching, laying out when a conflict might be morally acceptable. But many senior figures now argue that the current war doesn’t meet those criteria.

Voices within the US Church have echo

title: Why Trump’s Iran war feels different from past US conflicts
content:
What looks like a more aggressive war strategy is also revealing something deeper about how America now fights and understands war.

Why Trump’s Iran war feels different from past US conflicts

More than a month after launching airstrikes on Iran, Donald Trump finally addressed Americans from the White House. But instead of laying out a clear strategy, the speech drifted into comparisons with past wars, almost as if to reassure people that 32 days of conflict was still “early.”

That framing stood out. Not because of the history lesson itself, but because it treated war like a neat timeline with a clear beginning, middle and end. That’s not really how things have been unfolding, the New York Times reported.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

In recent months, US actions have felt scattered and overlapping rather than part of a single, defined conflict. Moves involving Iran, tensions around Venezuela, talk of Greenland, pressure on Cuba — none of it has followed a straight narrative. Instead, it has felt closer to a rolling series of episodes, each one replacing the last.

When conflict starts to look like content

One of the most noticeable changes is in how this war is being shown to people.

The White House hasn’t just been briefing or updating, it’s been posting. Clips of airstrikes have been edited with movie-style effects, stitched together with video game visuals, and pushed out on social media in a way that feels closer to internet culture than anything we’ve seen in past conflicts.

For a while, that framing seemed to work. It kept things distant, almost abstract. But that distance doesn’t hold forever.

As the weeks have gone by, reality has started to cut through. Fuel prices creeping up, growing unease around the Strait of Hormuz, and a general sense that this might drag on longer than expected have begun to shift the mood.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Even people around Donald Trump are starting to sound less certain. The conversation has quietly moved from confidence to questions, especially around how this started and whether the risks were fully thought through.

The backdrop: America’s long era of “endless wars”

To really make sense of this moment, you have to zoom out a bit.

Since the early 2000s, particularly after the September 11 attacks, the US hasn’t really fought wars with clear finish lines. Instead, it’s been involved in conflicts that stretch on, shift shape, and rarely come to a clean end.

Most of these wars happen far away, and for most Americans, they barely touch daily life. That distance matters.

Over time, it’s changed how people react. There’s less urgency, less sense of personal stake, and often not much clarity on what “winning” even means anymore.

Different presidents have come and gone, but they’ve all operated within this same reality, managing ongoing conflicts rather than clearly starting or ending them.

Why Americans feel disconnected from these wars

A big part of thi

title: Why a major satellite firm is restricting images of Iran and the Middle East war
content:
Planet Labs says a US government request has led to tighter controls, raising questions about transparency during conflict.

Why a major satellite firm is restricting images of Iran and the Middle East war

Satellite imagery, one of the few ways to independently track what’s happening in conflict zones, is about to get harder to access.

Planet Labs has said it will indefinitely restrict access to images over Iran and the wider Middle East conflict region, citing a request from the US government.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The decision was shared with customers over the weekend and marks a significant shift in how much real-time visibility is available to journalists, researchers and the public, the New York Times reported.

What’s changed

Until recently, Planet Labs had already introduced a delay, holding back images from Iran for about two weeks before making them available.

Now, it’s going a step further.

The company says it will withhold imagery indefinitely and move to a more controlled system, releasing images only in specific cases, such as urgent needs or when it determines there is a clear public interest.

The restrictions apply to imagery collected since March 9 and cover Iran, nearby Gulf countries and active conflict zones.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Why this matters

Satellite imagery plays a critical role in modern conflict reporting.

It helps verify military activity, track damage on the ground and provide independent evidence in places where access is limited or information is tightly controlled.

For journalists and analysts, it’s often one of the few ways to piece together what’s actually happening.

Limiting that access makes it harder to build that picture.

What the company is saying

Planet Labs has described the move as a response to a US government request made for “safety and operational security reasons.”

It says the goal is to prevent imagery from being used in ways that could create risks during an active conflict, while still allowing for some level of controlled access.

The Pentagon, however, has not confirmed whether such a request was formally made.

A broader shift

Planet Labs isn’t alone. Other satellite companies have also introduced restrictions or delays on imagery from the region, though some say their policies are consistent with existing rules around sensitive areas.

Still, the overall trend is clear, access is becoming more limited.

The bigger picture

This raises a familiar tension. On one side is the need for operational security during a conflict. On the other is the role of transparency and independent verification.

Satellite imagery sits right in the middle of that balance.

For now, the shift suggests that in active war zones, control over information, even from space, is tightening.

title: Why Iran’s leadership has changed faces, not direction
content:
Trump says Iran has gone through regime change, but what has actually emerged looks less like a new system and more like a tougher, more security-driven version of the old one.

Why Iran’s leadership has changed faces, not direction

Donald Trump has been arguing that Iran has effectively gone through regime change because so many of its top figures have been killed and a new set of leaders is now in place. But that is not how most analysts would define it.

A real regime change means the system itself has been transformed. That has not happened in Iran. The Islamic Republic is still the same authoritarian theocracy created after the 1979 revolution. The power structure has not been dismantled. It has simply hardened, CNN reported.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

If anything, the war seems to have pushed more power toward the most hardline parts of the system, especially the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, or IRGC. So while the names at the top may have shifted, the direction of the regime does not appear more moderate. It looks sharper, more militarised and less willing to compromise.

The hardliners appear to be even stronger now

That is the real story here.

The emerging leadership is not made up of reformers or pragmatists suddenly taking over after a collapse. It is coming from the same security establishment that has long sat at the centre of Iran’s most repressive policies. And because the country is now under even more pressure, that security state is likely to tighten its grip rather than loosen it.

Mojtaba Khamenei may now be the central figure, but his rise does not suggest a break from the past. If anything, it suggests even closer alignment with the Revolutionary Guards, who are widely seen as the main force behind his elevation.

That matters because it shifts the balance of power further toward the military-security apparatus. In plain terms, Iran does not seem to be entering a softer phase. It may be entering a more rigid one.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Repression at home is likely to get worse

Wars do not just reshape foreign policy. They also change how regimes behave at home.

In Iran’s case, many analysts think the leadership will now be even more paranoid about unrest, dissent and any sign of weakness. A wounded regime that feels exposed does not usually become more open. It becomes more suspicious and more brutal.

That is why there is growing concern that repression will intensify. The people now gaining influence are not outsiders. They are figures with long records inside the security system, including domestic crackdowns. So the expectation is not reform, but tougher surveillance, more arrests and a heavier response to any protest movement.

The internet blackout, censorship and arrests of dissidents all point in that direction already. This no longer looks like a government trying to steady itself and reconnect with society. It looks like one trying to lock everything down.

title: Downed US pilot communicated with military hours after ejecting in Iran
content:
The weapons systems officer was reportedly wounded but still able to walk, and evaded Iranian forces in mountainous terrain for more than a day before being rescued.

The pilot was recovered within hours of the aircraft being shot down, while the second crew member remained missing until a longer search and extraction operation was completed. (Representative image)

US airman rescued after two days evading capture in Iran

Both crew members made contact after ejecting from the F-15

CIA ran deception campaign to mislead Iranian search teams Did our AI summary help?

The US airman whose fighter jet was shot down over Iranian territory reportedly made contact with the US military as early as Friday from behind enemy lines.

This development helped set in motion a complex rescue operation, according to the chief US security analyst at CNN, Jim Sciutto.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Sciutto, citing sources, said US forces established “early communications” with the stranded service member around noon Eastern Time on Friday, after which authorities immediately intensified efforts to locate and recover him.

“I was also told that he had been injured initially in the ejection, which of course presented other difficulties because they were concerned about getting him out, getting him out safely, being able to treat those injuries,” Sciutto said.

According to officials cited by Axios, both the pilot and the weapons systems officer were able to make contact via communications systems after ejecting from the F-15. The weapons systems officer was reportedly wounded but still able to walk, and evaded Iranian forces in mountainous terrain for more than a day before being rescued.

The pilot was recovered within hours of the aircraft being shot down, while the second crew member remained missing until a longer search and extraction operation was completed.

Sciutto described the broader mission as “remarkable,” noting that US aircraft conducting low-altitude search operations were exposed to potential hostile fire while attempting to locate the stranded airman.

Meanwhile, a senior administration official said the CIA carried out a deception campaign inside Iran prior to the rescue effort, claiming the agency “spread word inside Iran that U.S. forces had already found him and were moving him on the ground for exfiltration out of the country,” in an effort to mislead opposing search teams.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

As per officials, the airman evaded capture for nearly two days before being rescued in a coordinated operation involving intelligence, air power, and ground support.

Initial reports had indicated that during the initial rescue attempt for the first crew member, Iranian forces fired on US recovery aircraft, striking an A-10 Thunderbolt II attack aircraft and at least one rescue helicopter. The damaged A-10 was reportedly flown out of Iranian airspace before the pilot ejected, while officials said all rescue he

title: JASSM-ER stealth missile: The 600-mile long-range weapon whose entire inventory US is planning to deploy against Iran
content:
The Joint Air-to-Surface Standoff Missile–Extended Range, or JASSM-ER, is a stealthy, long-distance cruise missile built by Lockheed Martin for the US military.

US airmen transport a Joint Air-to-Surface Standoff Missile at Dyess Air Force Base, Texas. (Credit: Bloomberg)

US nearly depletes JASSM-ER missile stockpiles in Iran campaign

About 425 of 2,300 pre-war JASSM-ER missiles remain

Missile stockpiles may take years to rebuild at current output Did our AI summary help?

The United States is preparing to deploy a large share of its long-range JASSM-ER cruise missile inventory to its ongoing military operations against Iran, a move that is sharply reducing reserves originally intended for other regions.

Citing a source familiar with the development, a news report in the Economic Times citing a Bloomberg report said instructions were issued in late March to withdraw these high-value missiles—each costing roughly $1.5 million—from stockpiles in the Pacific. Additional supplies from the continental US and other locations are also being redirected to bases under US Central Command as well as to Fairford in the United Kingdom.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The Joint Air-to-Surface Standoff Missile–Extended Range, or JASSM-ER, is a stealthy, long-distance cruise missile built by Lockheed Martin for the US military. It is designed to hit strategic targets deep within hostile territory while bypassing sophisticated air defence systems.

With a strike range exceeding 600 miles, the weapon allows aircraft to engage targets from a safe distance, without entering heavily defended zones. It is commonly launched from bombers such as the B-1B Lancer and B-52 Stratofortress, as well as from certain fighter aircraft.

As The Economic Times explains, the JASSM-ER is an upgraded version of the original JASSM, which has a much shorter reach of about 250 miles. Its stealth profile and precision targeting capabilities make it particularly effective against fortified or high-risk objectives, reducing the need for aircraft to operate within contested airspace.

Unlike the Tomahawk missile, which is typically fired from naval platforms, the JASSM-ER is air-launched, offering greater flexibility in how and where it can be deployed.

Before the current conflict with Iran, the US was estimated to have a stockpile of around 2,300 JASSM-ER missiles. However, extensive operational use and redeployment have significantly reduced that number.

Following recent allocations, only about 425 missiles are expected to remain available for use, with an additional portion considered unserviceable due to technical limitations. At this level, the remaining inventory would be sufficient for only a limited number of bomber sorties.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

According to Economic Times, since 2009, the US has funded the acquisition of more than 6,200 JASSM variants in total, although production of the original model ended roughly a decade ago. Th

title: Fire erupts at Bapco oil storage facility in Bahrain after Iranian airstrikes | Watch
content:
The Interior Ministry said civil defence teams were deployed immediately after the incident, adding that “measures were taken to control a fire that broke out as a result of the Iranian aggression targeting one of the facilities.”

Earlier, on March 9, Bapco had declared force majeure on its operations following an attack on its refinery complex.

Fire at Bapco oil facility in Bahrain after Iranian airstrike

Blaze extinguished, no casualties reported, damage assessed

Domestic fuel supply remains secure despite disruption Did our AI summary help?

A fire broke out at an oil storage facility operated by Bapco Energies in Bahrain on Sunday following an Iranian airstrike. The blaze has since been brought under control and extinguished, confirmed officials.

The Interior Ministry said civil defence teams were deployed immediately after the incident, adding that “measures were taken to control a fire that broke out as a result of the Iranian aggression targeting one of the facilities.”

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Sirens were sounded in the area, with residents urged to remain calm and move to nearby safe locations as emergency crews responded.

Massive fire erupted at the Bapco oil refinery in Bahrain after it was hit by missiles and explosive drones. Follow: https://t.co/mLGcUTS2eipic.twitter.com/kGh30XzrKg — Press TV 🔻 (@PressTV) April 5, 2026

The state-run Bahrain News Agency later reported that the fire, which affected a warehouse linked to the energy company, had been fully extinguished. Authorities confirmed that no casualties were reported, while damage assessments at the site are currently underway.

Earlier, officials said the blaze had broken out at a Bapco facility without initially disclosing the exact location. Emergency teams were deployed to prevent the fire from spreading to other parts of the installation.

Earlier, on March 9, Bapco had declared force majeure on its operations following an attack on its refinery complex, further highlighting the growing disruption to energy infrastructure amid escalating regional tensions.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The company’s Sitra refinery, one of its key assets with a capacity of around 380,000 barrels per day, has also faced operational strain during the conflict.

Despite the disruption, Bapco officials have said domestic fuel supply remains secure, noting that contingency measures are in place to ensure continued availability of petroleum products in the local market and exports across the Middle East and Asia.

Authorities have not yet released full details on the scale of damage from the latest incident or confirmed the extent of operational impact.

title: Iranian media says IRGC downed US C-130 in Isfahan, five killed during search as Trump announces pilot rescue
content:
Tasnim news agency quoted the Guards as saying, "An American enemy aircraft that was searching for the pilot of a downed fighter jet was destroyed by the fighters of Islam in the southern region of Isfahan."

This handout photo provided by Iran's Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) official website Sepah News on April 5, 2026 reportedly shows the wreckage and remains of targeted and crashed aircraft in central Iran. Pic: AFP

Iran says police commandos destroyed US C-130 in rescue attempt

Trump says US rescued two pilots from Iran in risky operations

Strikes on Iran spark fears for global energy supplies Did our AI summary help?

Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said a US aircraft linked to a search effort was destroyed, Iranian media reported Sunday, amid competing narratives over a US rescue mission inside Iran.

Fars news agency said the aircraft, identified as a C-130, was brought down by a police commando unit called the Faraj Rangers. It claimed the plane was “refuelling the evil invaders of the sacred land of the homeland”.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Overnight strikes in southwestern Iran killed five people during efforts to recover a missing US crewmember from a downed fighter jet, Iranian media reported on Sunday.

“Five people were martyred in last night’s attack on the Kouh-e Siah area” in Kohgiluyeh and Boyer-Ahmad province, provincial official Iraj Kazemijou was quoted as saying by the Tasnim news agency.

He added that the deaths occurred as the United States was searching for “the pilot or pilots” of the crashed aircraft.

Watch: Downed US aggressor aircraft in southern Isfahan Follow: https://t.co/mLGcUTS2eipic.twitter.com/QHxtcRmuSU — Press TV 🔻 (@PressTV) April 5, 2026

Meanwhile, Donald Trump said earlier Sunday that U.S. forces had successfully recovered a second crew member from a fighter jet that crashed within Iranian territory, describing the effort as highly risky. He called it "one of the most daring Search and Rescue Operations in U.S. History".

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

This handout photo provided by Iran's Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) official website Sepah News on April 5, 2026 shows smoke billowing reportedly from the site of targeted and crashed aircraft in central Iran. AFP

In a post on Truth Social, Trump said the rescued service member was located deep inside hostile territory. "This brave Warrior was behind enemy lines in the treacherous mountains of Iran, being hunted down by our enemies," he said, adding, "He sustained injuries, but he will be just fine."

Trump also indicated that another pilot had been recovered earlier, but the operation had not been disclosed at the time. "This miraculous Search and Rescue Operation comes in addition to a successful rescue of another brave Pilot, yesterday, which we did not confirm, because we did not want to jeopardize our second rescue operation."

The developments come amid a widening conflict that has spread acros

title: 'Battle-tested generals refuse to be yes-men for TV host': Iran's Parliament Speaker slams Trump admin
content:
The comments come as US Secretary of War Pete Hegseth forced out two army generals in a major rejig after vowing for regime change in the Iran war, the Wall Street Journal reported

Iran has repeatedly threatened to close or restrict shipping through the Strait, where roughly 20% of the world's oil passes

Iran's Parliament Speaker MB Ghalibaf, on Sunday, praised battle-tested generals for prioritising national interests over external pressures, amidst tensions between the US and Iran.

In a post on X, he highlighted that the US grapples with decisions on supporting Israel and managing oil prices, potentially impacting American taxpayers in the long term.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

He said, "SITREP: Battle-tested generals refuse to be yes-men for a TV host selling out American lives for Israel's delusions. That's the short-term price. Long-term cost? American taxpayers paying at the pump for decades. Real LEGACY."

Ghalibaf is suggesting a rift between professional US military "generals" and the political appointees (like Hegseth) who he claims are "selling out" American interests for Israel's military goals. Ghalibaf is hinting at Iran's ability to disrupt global energy supplies.

Iran has repeatedly threatened to close or restrict shipping through the Strait, where roughly 20% of the world's oil passes. He is arguing that while the U.S. might see "short-term" military wins, the "long-term cost" for American taxpayers will be decades of high energy prices and economic instability due to the destruction of Middle Eastern energy infrastructure.

The comments come as US Secretary of War Pete Hegseth forced out two army generals in a major rejig after vowing for regime change in the Iran war, the Wall Street Journal reported.

General David Hodne, who became the head of the service's Training and Transformation Command in October, and Major General William Green Jr., Chief of Army Chaplains, were removed after General Randy George, the Army's chief of staff, as reported by the Wall Street Journal.

The removal comes late in a series of clashes between the Pentagon chief and the service's senior leadership. George has been asked to retire immediately, even though he was expected to hold the office for more than another year, until the fall of 2027, and complete what is typically a four-year assignment as a member of the Joint Chiefs of Staff.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Meanwhile, on March 28, demonstrators hit the streets of cities across the United States for the first "No Kings" protest since the joint US and Israeli war against Iran, as reported by Al Jazeera.

According to the "No Kings" website, more than 3,300 events were planned across all 50 states, with large crowds expected in cities such as New York, Los Angeles and Washington, DC. Parallel events happened internationally in cities such as Rome, Paris, and Berlin.

US and Israeli forces have been conducting strikes on Iranian targets for over a mon

title: US inflation seen spiking in first snapshot since Iran war
content:
Economists are penciling in a 1% increase in the consumer price index for March — the sharpest one-month advance since 2022

Combined with signs of stabilization in the US labor market, stubborn price pressures along with new inflation risks stemming from war in the Middle East help explain why the Fed may struggle to lower interest rates this year.

US inflation to surge amid Iran war and soaring gas prices

Fed may struggle to lower rates amid new inflation risks

Central banks worldwide likely to keep policy steady this week Did our AI summary help?

The sudden increase in US gasoline prices felt by American consumers is set to be on full display in key inflation data due out this coming week.

Economists are penciling in a 1% increase in the consumer price index for March — the sharpest one-month advance since 2022 — after the Iran war pushed gas prices at the pump up by about $1 per gallon.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

At the same time, the core CPI, excluding energy and food, probably rose 0.3% from a month earlier, according to a Bloomberg survey ahead of the Bureau of Labor Statistics report due Friday.

A day ahead of the CPI, the Federal Reserve’s preferred gauge of inflation will offer a snapshot of pre-war price pressures. Economists see the so-called core personal consumption expenditures (PCE) price index, which excludes food and energy, having risen by 0.4% for a third month in February, suggesting progress toward tamer inflation was stalling even before the conflict.

Combined with signs of stabilization in the US labor market, stubborn price pressures along with new inflation risks stemming from war in the Middle East help explain why the Fed may struggle to lower interest rates this year.

The mid-week release of minutes from the central bank’s March policy meeting may shed light on officials’ concerns about inflation or the potential economic impacts stemming from the Iran conflict and related disruptions to energy and other commodity flows.

In addition to the PCE price data, the Bureau of Economic Analysis’ report will include figures on personal spending as well as incomes. Economists expect a modest increase in inflation-adjusted spending.

Other reports in the coming week include the Institute for Supply Management’s March services activity index, due on Monday. And on Friday, the University of Michigan will issue its preliminary April consumer sentiment index.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

In Canada, the March labor force survey will offer a first look at how surging energy costs may be filtering through to job growth and unemployment. Economists expect the jobless rate to tick up to 6.8%.

Elsewhere, central banks from Poland to India and New Zealand may keep policy steady as they monitor events in the Middle East, while inflation gauges from China to Latin America will point to the impact on living costs.

title: Why first F-15E pilot rescue was kept secret? Trump reveals claiming air dominance over Iranian skies
content:
Trump disclosed that another pilot had already been rescued a day earlier, but officials chose not to make that information public at the time.

US President Donald Trump

Second F-15E crew member rescued after dramatic firefight

Earlier pilot rescue kept secret to protect ongoing mission

Both rescues completed with no American casualties Did our AI summary help?

US President Donald Trump has confirmed that a second crew member from a downed F-15E fighter jet has been recovered, while also revealing that an earlier rescue was deliberately kept undisclosed to avoid jeopardizing the ongoing mission.

In a post on Truth Social, Trump announced the latest development, declaring, “WE GOT HIM!” and describing the effort as “one of the most daring Search and Rescue Operations in U.S. History.” He said the rescued officer, identified only as a senior-ranking crew member, is now “SAFE and SOUND” despite sustaining injuries.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Trump disclosed that another pilot had already been rescued a day earlier, but officials chose not to make that information public at the time. “This miraculous Search and Rescue Operation comes in addition to a successful rescue of another brave Pilot, yesterday, which we did not confirm, because we did not want to jeopardize our second rescue operation,” he wrote.

The decision to withhold details underscores the risks surrounding the second mission, which was still underway when the first recovery occurred. According to reporting by Al Jazeera, the second crew member had been evading Iranian forces for nearly two days after the aircraft was shot down. The rescue unfolded under dangerous conditions, with correspondents describing a “dramatic firefight” during the operation.

While Trump’s statement suggested a completed and successful extraction, reporting from Al Jazeera indicated the situation remained fluid. A US official told the network that although the missing crew member had been located and recovered, the operation was not fully complete. The rescue team still needed to exit Iranian territory safely, meaning both the airman and the personnel involved could remain at risk.

“There was a fierce firefight,” Al Jazeera reported, citing a US government source, adding that the mission was “ongoing” and that safe exfiltration had not yet been confirmed at that stage.

Trump framed the dual rescues as a demonstration of US military strength, emphasizing that both missions were carried out without casualties. “The fact that we were able to pull off both of these operations, without a SINGLE American killed, or even wounded, just proves once again, that we have achieved overwhelming Air Dominance and Superiority over the Iranian skies,” he said.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The contrasting accounts highlight both the complexity of the operation and the sensitivity of releasing information during active military engagements, particularly when personnel remain inside

title: US rescues second F-15E crew member in Iran in 'daring' mission
content:
The rescued crew member, whose identity has not been disclosed, was one of two aircrew aboard the F-15E Strike Eagle shot down by Iranian forces on Friday.

A specialised commando unit conducted the operation with air cover, and all personnel involved were said to have exited Iranian airspace safely. (Representative image)

US forces rescued a stranded American airman in Iran

The operation was a high-risk mission with air support

All personnel exited Iranian airspace safely Did our AI summary help?

US forces have rescued the second American airman stranded in Iran in a high-risk operation early, after the crew member spent more than 24 hours inside hostile territory, according to US officials.

The rescue was carried out by a specialised commando unit with air support, in what officials described as a “daring” mission.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

All personnel involved in the operation have safely exited Iranian airspace, they said.

“WE GOT HIM!” an elated US President Donald Trump announced in a statement Sunday morning, adding that the officer, described as a “highly respected Colonel,” is now “SAFE and SOUND.”

The aviator, whose identity has not yet been disclosed, was one of two crew members aboard an F-15E Strike Eagle fighter jet that was shot down by Iranian forces on Friday. The second crew member was rescued shortly after the crash and is reported to be safe and receiving medical attention.

The operation followed an intensive search effort launched immediately after the aircraft went down, with US forces racing to locate and extract the missing airman from difficult terrain.

According to The Wall Street Journal, which cited two US officials, the extraction involved a coordinated assault supported by air cover.

Meanwhile, according to Iranian state media, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said it had “destroyed” the US aircraft that was taking part in operations to locate the downed F-15E crew member, reported AFP.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

"An American enemy aircraft that was searching for the pilot of a downed fighter jet was destroyed by the fighters of Islam in the southern region of Isfahan," the Tasnim news agency cited the Guards.

However, there has been no immediate confirmation from Washington on this.

The successful rescue comes less than two days before Trump’s deadline for Iran to negotiate a deal with the U.S. or reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

title: Satellite firm Planet Labs to indefinitely withhold Iran war images- Moneycontrol.com
content:
California-based Planet Labs announced the decision in an email to customers and said the US government had asked all satellite imagery providers to indefinitely withhold images of the conflict region

A satellite image shows Iran's Law Enforcement Command (FARAJA) in Tehran, Iran, March 3, 2026, amid the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran. 2026 Planet Labs PBC/Handout via REUTERS/File Photo

Planet Labs to indefinitely halt satellite imagery of Iran

US orders providers to limit imagery of conflict regions

Images released individually for urgent or public-interest needs Did our AI summary help?

Satellite imaging firm Planet Labs said on Saturday it will indefinitely withhold visuals of Iran and the region of conflict in the Middle East to comply with a request from the U.S. government.

California-based Planet Labs announced the decision in an email to customers and said the U.S. government had asked all satellite imagery providers to indefinitely withhold images of the conflict region.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

The restriction expands upon a 14-day delay on imagery of the Middle East that Planet Labs imposed last month, a move the firm said was meant to prevent adversaries from using it to attack the U.S. and its allies.

Planet Labs said it will withhold imagery dating back to March 9 and that it expects the policy to remain in effect until the conflict ends.

The war began when the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran on February 28, and the conflict spread in the region when Tehran responded by launching its own attacks on Israel and U.S. bases in Gulf states, including Saudi Arabia, Kuwait and Bahrain.

Military uses of satellite technology include target identification, weapons guidance, missile tracking and communications. Some space specialists say Iran could be accessing commercial imagery, including pictures obtained via U.S. adversaries. Satellite images also help journalists and academicians studying hard-to-reach places.

Planet Labs, which operates a large fleet of Earth-imaging satellites and sells frequently updated images to governments, companies and media, did not respond to a request for further comment.

The Pentagon said it does not comment on intelligence-related matters.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Planet Labs said in its email to customers that it would switch to a "managed distribution of images" deemed not to pose a risk to safety. Under a new system, Planet Labs will release imagery on a case-by-case basis for urgent, mission-critical requirements or in the public interest.

"These are extraordinary circumstances, and we are doing all we can to balance the needs of all our stakeholders," the firm said.

One commercial provider, Vantor, formerly Maxar Technologies, told Reuters that it was not contacted by the U.S. government. Vantor for years has reserved the right to "implement enhanced access controls during times of geopolitical conflict" and currently has applied them for parts of

title: HLS: Drone strike hits Kuwait Petroleum HQ as Iran widens Gulf attacks
content:
The escalation came as Iran launched fresh missile and drone attacks on Israel and Kuwait early Sunday, in response to ongoing US-Israeli strikes.

Hours before the attack, Iran’s semi-official Fars news agency announced an updated “target list” that included electrical, water and steam infrastructure, in addition to the oil, natural gas and chemical assets that already have been under fire.

Drone strike hit Kuwait Petroleum HQ, causing fire and evacuation

Iran intensified attacks on Gulf energy infrastructure Sunday

Conflict disrupts energy markets, raising fears of regional war Did our AI summary help?

A drone strike hit the headquarters of Kuwait Petroleum Corporation early Sunday, sparking a fire. The building, which also houses the emirate’s oil ministry, was evacuated as Iran stepped up attacks on Gulf energy infrastructure.

Firefighting teams were deployed to the site, while officials said damage assessments were underway.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

“The oil sector leadership is closely monitoring the assessment of damages… while taking all necessary measures to ensure the safety of personnel and secure the site,” the company said in a statement.

The strike followed a series of recent aerial attacks targeting Kuwait’s key energy facilities, including the Mina Al-Ahmadi and Mina Abdullah refineries, as well as repeated strikes on the country’s airport.

Hours before the latest attack, Iran’s semi-official Fars news agency published an updated “target list” that expanded beyond oil and gas facilities to include electricity, water and steam infrastructure. The list also named industrial assets such as Kuwait’s PIC fertilizer and polymer manufacturer.

The escalation came as Iran launched fresh missile and drone attacks on Israel and Kuwait early Sunday, in response to ongoing US-Israeli strikes.

Both countries said their air defence systems were engaged in intercepting incoming threats.

The conflict, now over a month old, has spread across the Middle East, disrupting global energy markets and threatening shipping through the critical Strait of Hormuz.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

A US- or Israeli-linked strike on a petrochemical hub in southwestern Iran killed at least five people on Saturday, according to local officials.

US President Donald Trump renewed pressure on Tehran, warning it had 48 hours to agree to a deal or face severe consequences.

Iran’s central military command rejected the ultimatum, with General Ali Abdollahi Aliabadi calling it a “helpless, nervous, unbalanced and stupid action,” and warning in response that “the gates of hell will open.”

Strikes have increasingly targeted economic and industrial infrastructure across the region. US-Israeli attacks hit a cement plant and a trade terminal on the Iran-Iraq border, while Iran retaliated with drone and missile strikes across the Gulf.

title: How Iran’s downing of US jets punctures Trump’s aura of invincibility
content:
Trump has repeatedly claimed dominance over Iranian airspace and used maximalist rhetoric to suggest the US has won

Trump has dangled the possibility of the war ending shortly with a claim of victory as a means to calm markets and reassure nervous members of Congress

Iran downs US jet, casting doubt on Trump’s victory claims

Trump faces political backlash as Americans oppose the Iran war.

Loss of US aircraft raises doubts about Trump's air supremacy. Did our AI summary help?

The downing of a US aircraft and the dayslong search for a missing crew member has pierced the aura of invincibility that President Donald Trump has sought to project as he tries to stave off the increasing political risks of the Iran war he started five weeks ago alongside Israel.

Trump has repeatedly claimed dominance over Iranian airspace and used maximalist rhetoric to suggest the US has won and that Iran’s military capability has been eliminated in an effort to calm markets and an American public that is strongly opposed to the war.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

“They have no anti-aircraft protection. They have no nothing. They don’t have anything,” Trump told reporters on Tuesday. “They’re not putting up a fight. They’re not even shooting at us, OK?”

Days later, on Friday, Iran shot down an American F-15E fighter jet, raising questions about Trump’s declaration of near-victory in the war the US and Israel launched some five weeks ago. An A-10 Warthog plane crashed in the Persian Gulf the same day, reportedly after being hit by enemy fire.

“This is just another indication that Iran has lots of cards that they continue to play,” said retired Brigadier General Steve Anderson. “Obviously that puts us at risk.”

Although analysts praised the work of the US military, they said the shooting cast doubt on Trump’s claim of air supremacy over the Middle Eastern country.

“I think he’s going to have a harder time now at least convincing the American people that Iran has been totally destroyed,” said Republican former US Representative Adam Kinzinger, a former Air Force pilot and longtime Trump critic.

“It’s definitely going to hurt him politically because it gives more fodder to those who say he went in there recklessly,” he said.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

In a primetime speech to the nation on Wednesday night, Trump said the US would conclude the war in two to three weeks. But he has vacillated between saying that the US was enjoying victory and would leave opening the Strait of Hormuz to other countries and threatening to bomb civilian sites — a war crime under the Geneva Convention — if Iran didn’t do so on its own.

“Never in the history of warfare has an enemy suffered such clear and devastating large-scale losses in a matter of weeks,” he said. “Our enemies are losing and America, as it has been for five years under my presidency, is winning, and now winning bigger than ever before.”

But continuing the contradictions that hav

title: Iranian security official warns of 'big surprise' for US-Israel amid escalating conflict
content:
The comments come as the conflict, now over a month old, continues to widen. Iran launched missiles and drones early Sunday targeting Israel and Kuwait, with both countries activating air defence systems in response.

He further criticised US President Donald Trump’s messaging, suggesting it was aimed at shaping perception rather than reflecting battlefield realities

Iran warns of a "big surprise" for US-Israeli aggressors soon

Missile, drone strikes hit Israel, Kuwait as tensions rise

Iran claims control over Strait of Hormuz, key energy route Did our AI summary help?

A senior Iranian security official has warned that Tehran is preparing a “big surprise” for what he described as US-Israeli aggressors, as hostilities intensify across the region following fresh missile and drone exchanges.

In remarks to Lebanon’s Al-Mayadeen television network, the official reportedly said Iran was advancing according to a defined strategy and a pre-determined list of objectives.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

“We have a big surprise for the US and the Israeli regime; it only takes a little time,” he said, adding that recent developments could work to Tehran’s advantage. “Of course, their action in southern Iran can turn time in our favor.”

The official dismissed Washington’s assertions about the flexibility of its military targets, calling such claims irrelevant. “This is a meaningless word in military affairs,” he said, arguing that the US “bank of targets is not precise,” while Iran, he maintained, is proceeding “exactly according to its plans and its bank of targets.”

He further criticised US President Donald Trump’s messaging, suggesting it was aimed at shaping perception rather than reflecting battlefield realities.

“Given the failure of US military operations against Iran… Trump has resorted to the media to compensate for the US weakness in the battlefield,” the official said, also mocking references to civilian infrastructure. “That is ridiculous.”

The comments come as the conflict, now over a month old, continues to widen. Iran launched missiles and drones early Sunday targeting Israel and Kuwait, with both countries activating air defence systems in response, reported AFP. The attacks followed a renewed ultimatum from Trump, who warned Tehran it had “48 hours before all Hell will reign (sic) down on them” if it failed to reach a deal.

Iranian military figures have rejected the warning in strong terms. General Ali Abdollahi Aliabadi described the ultimatum as a “helpless, nervous, unbalanced and stupid action,” cautioning in turn that “the gates of hell will open for you.”

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Strikes and counter-strikes have increasingly focused on economic and strategic sites. Iranian forces have also emphasized their control over the Strait of Hormuz, a key global energy route, while targeting what they describe as economic assets linked to US and Israeli interests in the Gulf.

The Iranian official

title: 'If you import third world, you become third world', says US President Trump
content:
The comment, part of Trump’s broader immigration rhetoric, came alongside a sharp ultimatum directed at Tehran.

US President Donald Trump

Trump issues ultimatum to Iran, warns of military consequences

Iran launches missiles at Israel and Kuwait, rejects US ultimatum

Strikes near Bushehr nuclear plant prompt Russian evacuation Did our AI summary help?

US President Donald Trump stirred controversy with a post on Truth Social declaring, “If you import The Third World, you become The Third World!”, a remark that he posted even as he escalated pressure on Iran with a stark military warning.

The comment, part of Trump’s broader immigration rhetoric, came alongside a sharp ultimatum directed at Tehran. Trump said Iran had 48 hours to negotiate or face consequences, writing, “Time is running out -- 48 hours before all Hell will reign (sic) down on them.” The warning followed an earlier demand linked to reopening the Strait of Hormuz, a key global energy route.

Story continues below Advertisement Remove Ad

Within hours, Iran launched missiles and drones targeting Israel and Kuwait, with both nations activating air defence systems, according to AFP. The latest attacks are part of a broader confrontation that began over a month ago after US-Israeli strikes triggered retaliation from Tehran, steadily expanding into a region-wide conflict.

Rejecting the ultimatum, Iran’s central military command responded with equally sharp language. General Ali Abdollahi Aliabadi called Trump’s warning a “helpless, nervous, unbalanced and stupid action,” adding that “the gates of hell will open for you,” echoing the US president’s phrasing.

Trump later posted a video claiming significant damage in Tehran, stating, “Many of Iran's Military Leaders...are terminated, along with much else, with this massive strike in Tehran,” though he did not specify when the strike occurred.

Meanwhile, battlefield claims remain contested. Iranian authorities said they had downed US aircraft, including an F-15 and an A-10, with reports suggesting one crew member is still missing. Local officials indicated that search efforts involve both military personnel and civilians, describing the operation as “still ongoing,” and alleging that residents fired at US helicopters attempting to land.

Concerns have also mounted over strikes near sensitive infrastructure. An attack close to the Bushehr nuclear plant killed a guard, prompting Russia to evacuate workers and condemn the incident as “an evil deed.” Iran warned that continued targeting of the facility could lead to radioactive fallout affecting Gulf capitals.

Economic sites have increasingly become targets. US-Israeli strikes hit petrochemical and industrial facilities, while Iranian retaliation caused injuries and structural damage in Gulf states, including Bahrain and Dubai.

title: Gold, Silver Rates Today (Apr. 2) - Highlights: Gold slips 3.46% below Rs 1.49 lakh, silver sheds 6.72% below Rs 2.28 lakh; precious metals outlook
content:
IBJA rates: gold dips below Rs 1.50 lakh, silver down 6.19 percent; why is bullion down?

The Indian Bullion Jewellers Association pegged the standard price of 10 grams of gold at Rs 1,49,569, which is a 2.72 percent decline from the previous session close at Rs 1,45,507. Silver, too, declined 6.19 percent from Rs 2,39,483 to Rs 2,24,660 per kilogram at its 12:30 rate session. According to the Augmont report (Apr. 2), bullion witnessed significant profit-booking after reaching fresh two-week highs near $4,800 and $75, respectively, amid renewed escalation in the US–Iran conflict. The prolonged nature of the conflict, coupled with Donald Trump’s hawkish remarks, has reduced expectations of near-term de-escalation, thereby reinforcing the US Dollar’s dominance as a global reserve currency and exerting downward pressure on precious metals.

title: 'Going to hit them extremely hard in the next 2-3 weeks': Trump's stern warning for Iran
content:
"We are going to hit them extremely hard over the next two to three weeks. We are going to take them back to the stone ages where they belong,” Trump said in his address.

US President Donald Trump.
